items.title: Kimi Agent Swarm: when one model becomes a team
items.description:
Native orchestration for parallel agent execution — and where it fits in your stack

1. The single-agent bottleneck

Most agent frameworks today run one model instance through a loop: plan, act, observe, repeat. This works for tasks with clear sequential dependencies — write code, run tests, fix errors. But it breaks when the task has parallelizable subproblems that require different specializations.

Consider a practical example: match one candidate CV against 100 job descriptions, generating a tailored resume for each role. A single agent must process descriptions one by one. At 100 iterations, latency becomes unacceptable. At 1,000, it becomes unusable.

The standard workaround is external orchestration — LangGraph, CrewAI, or custom Python workflows. You define the graph, state transitions, retry logic, and arbitration rules. This gives full control, but adds engineering overhead: infrastructure to build, debug, and maintain.

Kimi K2.6 takes a different path. It embeds swarm orchestration into the model itself.

2. What Agent Swarm changes

Kimi K2.6 can deploy up to 300 sub-agents in parallel, executing across 4,000 coordinated steps in a single autonomous run. This is a 3× increase from K2.5’s 100 agents and 1,500 steps.

The key difference is not the ceiling. It is that the decomposition, assignment, and aggregation happen inside the model’s reasoning loop — not in external code you write.

You make one API call with the goal. Kimi decides:
- How to break the task into parallelizable subtasks
- Which agent profile fits each subtask
- How to manage dependencies and shared state
- How to synthesize results into a unified output

For tasks with independent parallel workstreams, this achieves up to 4.5× speedup compared to sequential execution.

3. Three use cases that show the pattern

Moonshot has demonstrated concrete workloads that illustrate what this architecture enables:

Resume matching at scale. One uploaded CV was matched against 100 relevant roles in California. The swarm delivered 100 fully customized resumes in one run — each tailored to the specific job description.

Landing pages from maps data. Kimi identified 30 retail stores in Los Angeles without websites, then generated high-converting landing pages for each, including booking functionality synchronized to a database.

Research paper generation. An uploaded astrophysics paper was converted into a reusable skill. The swarm then produced a 40-page, 7,000-word research paper with a structured dataset of 20,000+ entries and 14 charts.

These are not demos. They are evidence that the swarm handles genuinely heterogeneous work: broad search, deep research, large-scale analysis, long-form writing, and multi-format content generation in a single coordinated execution.

4. Architecture: why native multimodal matters

Kimi K2.6 is built on a 1-trillion parameter Mixture-of-Experts architecture, activating 32 billion parameters per token. This keeps inference costs manageable while maintaining frontier-level quality.

The native multimodal design is critical. Trained on ~15 trillion mixed visual and text tokens, K2.6 processes text and images as a single modality from the start. The 400M-parameter MoonViT vision encoder enables agentic tasks that require visual understanding: analyzing UI mockups, processing screenshots, replicating website journeys from images.

This matters for the swarm because real-world tasks are rarely text-only. A sub-agent analyzing a competitor’s landing page needs to see the layout. A sub-agent debugging a UI needs to compare the mockup with the implementation. Native multimodal support means these agents do not need external vision pipelines — they see and reason in one pass.

5. Document-to-skill: reusable patterns at scale

K2.6 can convert any high-quality document into a reusable skill. PDFs, spreadsheets, slides, Word documents — the model captures their structural and stylistic DNA.

This is more powerful than simple template matching. Instead of prompting “write a report like this,” you upload the report. Kimi extracts the pattern: section structure, data visualization style, argument flow. Future tasks inherit that quality without starting from scratch.

When combined with Agent Swarm, your best work becomes infrastructure. A well-designed project template propagates across 300 parallel subtasks, ensuring consistency at scale.

6. Claw Groups: heterogeneous open swarms

The most significant architectural addition is Claw Groups. This extends the swarm beyond Kimi-native agents to heterogeneous agents — other models, local deployments, mobile agents — all collaborating in a shared operational space.

The design principle is explicit: multiple agents and humans operate as genuine collaborators. You onboard agents from any device, each with their own toolkits and persistent memory contexts. K2.6 serves as the adaptive coordinator, matching tasks to agents based on their specific skill profiles.

Moonshot uses this internally for content production: Demo Makers, Benchmark Makers, Social Media Agents, and Video Makers working in parallel, with K2.6 coordinating the pipeline.

This represents a shift from “AI does tasks for you” to “AI coordinates a team of heterogeneous agents on your behalf.”

7. Benchmarks that matter

On agentic evaluation, K2.6 delivers competitive results:

- Humanity’s Last Exam (Full) with tools: 54.0% — leading GPT-5.4 (52.1%), Claude Opus 4.6 (53.0%), and Gemini 3.1 Pro (51.4%)
- BrowseComp (Agent Swarm mode): 86.3% vs. 78.4% for K2.5
- SWE-Bench Verified: 80.2%
- SWE-Bench Pro: 58.6

HLE-Full with tools is particularly significant because it tests autonomous resource utilization — exactly what Agent Swarm is designed for.

8. Where it breaks

No honest assessment skips the failure modes. Three are worth understanding:

State drift. Over long runs, sub-agents can develop inconsistent views of shared state. Agent A modifies a data structure; Agent B operates on a stale snapshot. The shared operational space is designed to prevent this, but the specific consistency guarantees are not publicly documented. For coding tasks where multiple agents modify the same codebase concurrently, the risk of breaking invariants established by another agent is real.

Coordinator saturation. When the coordinator is busy re-planning a stalled subtask, it may deprioritize status updates from other agents. The coordinator is K2.6 itself, and its capacity is finite. Under high load, the entire swarm slows.

Cost scaling. A 300-agent swarm with 8,000 output tokens each consumes 2.4 million output tokens per run. At K2.6 API pricing, that is approximately $7.20 per execution. For tasks requiring multiple iterations, costs can exceed $50–100. Automatic caching of shared system prompts reduces input costs by 75–83%, but output token costs do not benefit from caching.

9. The control tradeoff

Agent Swarm represents a specific architectural choice.

K2.6 Agent Swarm is model-native orchestration. One API call, one synthesized output. Zero infrastructure overhead. Zero ability to audit or customize the orchestration logic.

External orchestration (LangGraph, custom frameworks) gives full control over graph structure, state transitions, retry logic, and arbitration. Every agent turn is logged and inspectable. The cost is engineering effort to build and maintain the orchestration layer.

The choice is not about which is universally better. It is about where you want the control boundary.

Teams that must explain agent decisions — to auditors, security reviewers, or colleagues debugging failures — will find external orchestration’s transparency worth the setup cost. Teams that need to move fast on parallelizable tasks with minimal infrastructure will find Agent Swarm’s convenience hard to beat.

In our own agent platform, we see a similar pattern: native orchestration works well for bounded, parallelizable workflows. External orchestration is necessary when agent decisions must be traceable to specific business rules.

10. Bottom line

Kimi K2.6’s Agent Swarm is not a replacement for orchestration frameworks. It is a zero-infrastructure alternative for tasks that benefit from parallel specialization.

If your workflow involves independent subtasks that require different skills — research, writing, analysis, generation — and you do not need fine-grained control over the coordination logic, Agent Swarm removes the engineering tax of building that orchestration yourself.

If you need auditability, custom retry policies, or integration with non-Kimi agents in a deterministic graph, external orchestration remains the right tool.

Choose based on where you need control, not based on which approach is newer.

Daniel Braz is CTO/CPO at BRQ Digital Products Studio, shipping production agents and writing on agentic software engineering.

Kimi Agent Swarm: when one model becomes a team was originally published in Level Up Coding on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: Six Dimensions for Assessing AI Agent Maturity
items.description:
A practical framework for answering the question product and engineering teams actually face: where is this agent today, and what should we build next?

Several useful models exist for thinking about AI agent maturity. SAE-style level ladders (L0 through L5) give executives a shared vocabulary. Anthropic’s Building Effective Agents distinguishes workflows from agents at the architectural level. Karpathy’s autonomy slider frames the human-AI handoff as a continuous, user-controlled dimension. OpenAI’s 5 Levels of AGI charts where the underlying technology is heading over the next decade. Each answers a real question.

What I have found missing, when working with teams deploying agents in production, is a framework for a more operational question: given this specific use case, where is our agent today, and what do we invest in next? That question is harder than it looks, because “maturity” for an agent is not a single variable. It is the composition of several independent ones.

This piece proposes a six-dimension assessment I have been using for the last eighteen months with product and engineering teams. It complements the models above rather than replacing them. The existing maps tell you what shape agents can take and where the frontier is heading. This one tells you, for a specific agent you are building or operating, what to measure and where to improve.

The Starting Point

Before the dimensions, one observation worth stating plainly: maturity for an agent is a property of the system you have built, not of the model inside it. You can run a frontier reasoning model inside a deterministic workflow, or a small fine-tuned model inside a highly agentic loop. The model matters, but it matters less than the harness around it — the context engineering, the tool design, the verification layer, the way errors surface and recover.

This is why a single “how autonomous is it” score tends to underdetermine the answer. A system can be highly autonomous on one axis and deeply fragile on another, and the gap between “works in a demo” and “works in production” almost always lives in those gaps between axes. The framework below is designed to make those gaps visible.

Each dimension is independent. Each is assessed per use case, not per organization. A team can sit in very different positions on different agents they operate, and that is usually a sign of good engineering, not inconsistency.

The Six Dimensions

1. Task Structure

How decomposable is the problem into stable, predictable steps?

On one end, the task is a fixed pipeline — inputs flow through a predictable sequence of operations. On the other end, the task requires genuine dynamic planning over an open-ended search space, where the next step depends on what the previous one found.

This dimension matters because it tells you whether a workflow or an agent is the right architecture in the first place. Highly structured tasks rarely benefit from agentic flexibility; they benefit from reliability, latency, and cost predictability that workflows give you almost for free. Highly unstructured tasks, conversely, will suffocate inside a rigid pipeline.

Where to invest: if your task structure is high (very predictable), invest in workflow robustness — error handling, retries, observability. If it is low (genuinely dynamic), invest in the model’s planning surface and the quality of the tools it has to reach for.

2. Verification Cost

How fast and cheap is it to determine whether an output is correct?

This is, in my experience, the single most predictive variable for where agentic patterns succeed. Code has cheap verification — tests, compilers, type checkers, linters will tell you in seconds whether something works. Long-form writing has expensive verification — you have to read it carefully, and the errors are often subtle. Numerical analysis, legal drafting, medical triage each sit somewhere on that spectrum.

The reason this dimension dominates: agentic systems produce more outputs, faster, than anything that came before. If verification is cheap, that speed translates directly into value. If verification is expensive, every additional output the agent produces creates additional review burden — and at some point the agent becomes a net negative even though it is technically “more capable.”

Where to invest: when verification is expensive, the engineering work is on the verification side. Structured outputs, confidence signals, evaluator models, citations back to sources, visible reasoning traces, differential diffs — anything that compresses the time between “the agent said something” and “I know whether to trust it.”

3. Reversibility and Blast Radius

What does an error touch, and can it be undone?

A wrong suggestion in a code editor wastes thirty seconds. A wrong tool call in a customer-support agent might refund the wrong account. A wrong action in a production deployment pipeline might take down a service. A wrong action in a medical or financial system might be genuinely unrecoverable.

The acceptable level of autonomy is a function of this dimension more than of any other. Teams often get this backwards: they calibrate autonomy to how capable the model has become, when they should be calibrating it to what the system can break.

Where to invest: explicit reversibility primitives — dry-run modes, transactional tool calls, rollback hooks, staged approvals, sandboxed execution. If your blast radius is wide, your default posture should be “propose, do not execute” — even when the model is clearly capable of executing correctly most of the time.

4. Loop Speed

How fast is the generation-verification cycle?

Karpathy’s observation that this is the metric to optimize for partial autonomy is, in my view, one of the most important practical insights in the current generation of agent products. A system with lower autonomy but a sub-second loop consistently out-delivers a more autonomous system with a ten-minute loop, because the faster loop lets the human course-correct before drift compounds.

Loop speed is not just about latency. It is about how quickly a human (or another agent) can see what was produced, judge it, and feed the judgment back. A 200ms model response inside a 5-minute review cycle is a 5-minute loop. The slow step dominates.

Where to invest: instrument your full loop, not just the model call. Measure the time from “agent produced output” to “verifier (human or system) returned a signal.” Compress the slow step. This is where harness quality is most visible.

5. Context Persistence

How long does relevant context need to survive, and how reliably does it?

Some tasks fit in a single turn: one prompt, one response, done. Others require carrying context across hours (a multi-step research task), days (a product decision), weeks (a long-running project). As the horizon stretches, the probability that context degrades — gets forgotten, distorted, overwritten, or quietly replaced by plausible-sounding fabrications — grows.

Most production agents that strain on long-horizon work strain here, not on reasoning. The model can reason fine; the context underneath it has quietly rotted by turn 40.

Where to invest: at short horizons, the default context window is enough. At medium horizons, structured memory (summaries, pinned facts, skills files) pays off quickly. At long horizons, you need explicit persistence architecture — external memory stores, specification artifacts that anchor intent, and deliberate context-curation steps that refresh what the agent carries turn over turn.

6. Failure-Mode Tolerance

What happens, practically, when the system is wrong?

Related to blast radius but distinct. Blast radius asks “what can an error touch?” Failure-mode tolerance asks “how does the environment respond when an error happens?” A system where a wrong output triggers an obvious exception, surfaces clearly to the user, and is easy to retry has high failure-mode tolerance. A system where a wrong output silently corrupts downstream state — or looks identical to a correct one — has very low tolerance, and no amount of model capability will fix that.

This dimension determines how much margin for error the system has, which in turn sets a ceiling on how much autonomy is reasonable.

Where to invest: observability, clear failure signals, graceful degradation, the ability to distinguish “the agent refused” from “the agent failed silently” from “the agent succeeded but was wrong.” Visible failure is always better than invisible success that turns out to be wrong.

Using the Framework

The framework is not a score you aggregate into a single number. It is a profile — six positions, one per dimension, read together.

For assessment, a simple exercise: for each agent you operate, place a mark from low to high on each dimension based on the task’s character (for 1, 2, 3, 5, 6) or your current system’s position (for 4). The resulting profile tells you what kind of system you have.

For prioritization, look for mismatches. High blast radius with low failure-mode tolerance is a risk that any capability upgrade will amplify. Expensive verification with a slow loop will bottleneck on review no matter how fast the model gets. Long context persistence requirements without explicit memory architecture will quietly degrade over weeks.

For roadmapping, the question stops being “what level are we at?” and becomes “which dimension, if we moved it, would change the system the most?” Usually one or two stand out. That is the next investment.

For communication with stakeholders, the profile is easier to explain than a level. “Our support agent has cheap verification, narrow blast radius, and a tight loop — so we can push autonomy. Our deployment agent has wide blast radius and low failure-mode tolerance — so we keep it advisory, even though the model could clearly execute.” That is a conversation a CTO, a product lead, and a risk officer can have together.

Where Production Value Lives

When I overlay this framework against agents actually working in production — coding assistants, research tools, customer-support triage, document processing — a pattern repeats. The successful systems cluster in a recognizable region: cheap verification, narrow task scope, fast loops, tolerable failure modes, high reversibility, and a carefully engineered harness around a capable model. They are not maximally autonomous. They are well-fit.

The gap between a system that works and one that does not is rarely the model, especially now. It is the harness. The six dimensions are, in effect, six places where harness quality becomes visible. That is the practical value of the framework: it points you at where the engineering work actually is.

A Starting Point, Not a Final Word

I keep this framework deliberately lightweight. Six dimensions, assessed per use case, no aggregation, no arrow pointing anywhere. It is an assessment instrument, not a destiny.

The models it complements — the ladders, the slider, the capability roadmaps — each have their place. Use a ladder when you need a shared vocabulary across a large organization. Use the slider when you are designing the user surface of a specific product. Use OpenAI’s levels when you are thinking about where the technology is going and how to position for it.

And when the question is the more mundane, more immediate, more consequential one — where is our agent today, and what do we build next — use dimensions.

That is the question most teams actually need to answer. And the answer is almost never a single number.

Daniel Braz is CTO at BRQ Product & Experience Studios, where he leads a digital products studio focused on AI-augmented software engineering. He is currently researching context degradation and specification-driven development in agentic workflows.

Six Dimensions for Assessing AI Agent Maturity was originally published in Level Up Coding on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: The anatomy of an agentic platform
items.description:
Six layers, the boundaries between them, and why the agent builder is a surface — not the thing itself.

The phrase “agentic platform” collapses three different things into one word. There is the model — a weights file you call over HTTPS. There is the framework that wraps the model with tool calling, memory, and a loop: LangChain, LangGraph, Eino, the Agents SDK. And there is the platform — the long-lived system that runs agents in production, owns their lifecycle, bounds their behavior, accounts for their cost, and integrates them into a product that other humans or systems depend on.

Most of the conversations I see treat these three as interchangeable. They are not. A framework is a library that disappears from the problem the moment you deploy. A platform is a system with tenants, quotas, observability, governance, state, and a blast radius. An “agent builder” — the visual-or-config-driven authoring surface most vendors sell today — is neither of those things. It is a layer on top of a platform. When it is sold as the thing itself, it usually means someone else’s layers 1 through 6 are quietly running your business.

This article is a map. I walk the layers in the order they load at runtime, name the interface that actually matters at each one, and place the agent builder where it belongs: at the top, as one surface among several.

Why this map matters before any framework choice

Before the layers, one orientation. When you adopt a framework — LangGraph, Eino, the OpenAI or Anthropic Agents SDK, Google’s ADK, or something you wrote in an afternoon — you are implicitly accepting that framework’s opinion about which of these layers it owns, which it leaves to you, and where its boundaries are drawn. Most of the pain in agentic systems six months into production is not about model quality. It is about layers the framework quietly claimed that you needed to own, or layers it left to you that you didn’t realize existed.

The map is diagnostic. You can put any framework — or your own platform — next to it and see the gaps.

Layer 1: the runtime

The runtime is the loop. Call the model. Parse the response. If there are tool calls, execute them. Append the results. Loop. Return when the agent produces a final output or hits a turn limit.

That description is deceptively small. What actually lives here:

  • Provider abstraction: OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Bedrock, local models via Ollama or vLLM, each with slightly different tool-call semantics and streaming shapes.
  • Streaming: tokens, tool-call deltas, partial JSON, backpressure.
  • Cancellation and timeouts that propagate through tool calls.
  • Retry and backoff distinguishing retryable errors (rate limits, 5xx) from non-retryable ones (content policy, invalid tool schema).
  • Turn accounting and a bounded loop so an agent can’t spin indefinitely.

A runtime contract that survives contact with production is narrower than most frameworks make it. Something like:

Runtime:
  Run(agent: AgentSpec, input: Input) → RunResult | Error
  Stream(agent: AgentSpec, input: Input) → Stream<Event>

Notice what’s missing: no mention of tools, memory, or orchestration. Those are parameters to the AgentSpec or separate layers, not runtime concerns. Keeping the runtime narrow is what lets the rest of the platform evolve. A runtime that reaches up into context management, or sideways into tool dispatch, is a runtime you can't swap, instrument, or constrain independently — and those are exactly the operations you need to perform most often in production.

Layer 2: the context engine

Context engineering is now a legitimate sub-discipline, and it lives here — in the layer that assembles the messages array (and tool list, and system prompt) you send to the model on every turn. This is distinct from memory, which is about storage. Context is about what you send.

A context engine owns at least four responsibilities:

  1. Assembly: stitching together system prompt, persistent memories, retrieved knowledge, recent conversation, tool definitions, and any injected structured data. Order matters — model behavior is sensitive to where information sits in the context window.
  2. Budgeting: tracking token consumption per section and enforcing limits. A conversation that grows unchecked degrades faster than most teams expect.
  3. Compression and eviction: summarizing older turns, dropping low-value retrieval hits, collapsing redundant tool results. The naive “just truncate from the top” strategy is a source of silent failures.
  4. Isolation: when multiple agents or sub-agents share a conversation, deciding what each sees. Shared-everything is a context rot generator.

Most frameworks treat context as implicit — you hand them messages and they hand you a response. Which is fine until you need to measure, bound, or audit what the model actually saw. At that point you need a first-class Context type with accessors for each section, token-aware budgets, and hooks for compression strategies.

This is the layer most undersupplied by current tooling across every ecosystem, and also the layer where owning it yields the most leverage in production. If I were starting a platform today and had to choose one layer to build well and one to defer, I would build this layer well.

Layer 3: the tool plane

Tools are where an agent touches the world. The tool plane answers three questions: what can the agent call, what happens when it calls, and what comes back.

The tool interface is one of the few places where a strongly typed contract does real work. You want schemas validated at compile time (or at registration time, if the host language is dynamic), structured inputs and outputs, and a clear place to attach permission scopes. A workable starting point:

Tool:
  name:        string
  description: string
  schema:      JSONSchema
  run(args: json) → ToolResult | Error

ToolResult:
  content:     ContentBlock[]   // text, image, structured data, refs
  is_error:    boolean
  metadata:    { tokens, latency, cost, ... }

What this layer needs to handle beyond the interface:

  • Registration and discovery, including tools sourced from MCP servers that arrive dynamically.
  • Permission scoping: a production agent in a multi-tenant system does not get every tool; it gets a curated set bounded by the caller’s identity, the workspace’s policy, and whatever the session authorized.
  • Sandboxing: tools that shell out, hit filesystems, or execute code need a blast radius.
  • Result serialization that plays well with context-window budgets. Dumping a 200KB database query result into the context is how you discover layer 2 was not designed right.

A useful distinction is between native tools (code you own, running in-process) and remote tools (MCP or HTTP endpoints). Both implement the same Tool interface; the adapter lives behind it. This is the right seam, because it lets orchestration, observability, and policy treat them uniformly.

Layer 4: memory and knowledge

Memory and knowledge are about persistence. Context was about what you sent this turn. Memory is what the system remembered between turns, sessions, and users.

I separate this into three concerns that often get conflated:

  • Short-term memory: the current conversation’s full history, before the context engine decides what to include. Usually a Postgres table.
  • Long-term memory: derived facts about the user, the tenant, prior decisions. Generated by background processes (summarization, extraction) and surfaced into context on demand.
  • Knowledge: documents, structured data, databases that the agent can retrieve from. Hybrid retrieval — dense vectors plus BM25 plus metadata filters — is table stakes at this point; vector-only search is a 2023 move.

This layer is well served by adapter-shaped libraries rather than end-to-end frameworks: a pgvector driver, a Weaviate or Qdrant client, a BM25 or OpenSearch integration. You assemble the retriever out of these. What you should not do is let a framework bundle memory, retrieval, and context assembly into a single opinionated pipeline, because that pipeline becomes the thing you can't debug at 2 AM.

One interface, again:

Retriever:
  retrieve(query: Query) → Chunk[]

Query carries the text, filters, limits, and whatever reranking signals you want to pass through. Chunk carries content, provenance, and score. Everything else — the embeddings, the hybrid scoring, the reranker, the cache — lives behind that seam.

Layer 5: orchestration

If you only ever ran one agent at a time, you wouldn’t need this layer. You would need everything above, and you would be fine. Orchestration exists because real systems run agents in relation to each other: a supervisor dispatching to specialists, a fan-out that evaluates five candidates in parallel, a handoff that routes a conversation from a triage agent to a billing agent, a critique loop where one agent reviews another’s output.

What this layer should own: agent topologies, message buses between agents, handoff semantics, budget sharing (cost, turns, time), and failure modes (what happens when a sub-agent times out while a supervisor is waiting). What it should not own: the loop (layer 1), the tools (layer 3), or the context assembly (layer 2). Keep orchestration narrow and it composes. Let orchestration bleed into the lower layers and you get the frameworks-eat-your-lunch problem LangGraph users know well.

The problem shape is familiar to anyone who has built concurrent systems: independent units of work, structured cancellation (when the supervisor gives up, its children give up), fan-out with aggregation, and the select-whichever-finishes-first pattern that shows up in speculative execution. The substrate matters here, because the more naturally your runtime models those patterns, the less orchestration code you write — and the less of it ends up looking like an accident.

Layer 6: the operations plane

Observability, evaluation, and governance are often treated as three layers. In practice they share so much plumbing that I treat them as one plane with three faces.

Observability is tracing, structured logs, and metrics. Every LLM call, every tool call, every retrieval, every handoff should emit a span in an OpenTelemetry trace. You want prompt and response capture (with PII redaction), latency, token counts, and cost per span. OTEL instrumentation is a solved problem across every major runtime; there is no excuse to skip it.

Evaluation is the harness that runs agents against reference cases and measures what changed. An agentic platform without an eval layer is a platform that finds regressions by hearing users complain. The eval layer shares data structures with observability — a trace is an eval case, if you persist it — which is why I treat them together.

Governance is the set of policies the platform enforces regardless of what the agent tries to do: authentication, authorization, rate limits, cost caps per tenant, content policy, audit logging, PII handling, data residency. In regulated environments — and Brazil’s LGPD is one — governance is not optional and not a bolt-on.

This is the plane where operational footprint determines whether the platform is affordable at scale. Every agent turn should produce a full trace. Every trace should be cheap enough to keep. Every policy should be enforceable without reaching around the runtime. If the operations plane is an afterthought, the platform ships — but it ships as something you can’t safely scale.

The agent builder: a surface, not a layer

We can finally place the agent builder. It is not layer 7. It is a surface — an authoring interface — that sits on top of layers 1 through 6. Its job is to let someone (a developer, a solutions engineer, an internal business user, or another agent) specify an agent without hand-wiring the runtime.

A single platform can — and usually should — expose multiple builders:

  • Code-first: typed specs (structs, classes, or schema-validated JSON/YAML) that describe an AgentSpec and get loaded by the runtime. This is the surface engineers actually use.
  • Config-first: a declarative format (TOML, YAML, or a small DSL) that non-engineers can edit with guardrails. This is what gets you the business-user story without committing to visual tooling.
  • Visual: a graph editor in a web UI, producing the same underlying spec. This is what demos well.
  • Generative: a model that writes the spec based on a natural-language description, then hands it to the runtime. This is where the market is moving, and it is converging fast. Wonderful’s “Agent Builder,” launched in January 2026 in partnership with Anthropic, is one instantiation: an autonomous agent whose job is to build, test, and refine other agents on the same underlying platform. Sierra’s Ghostwriter, announced in March 2026, is another — and Sierra was explicit that supporting it required rearchitecting the platform as headless infrastructure so an agent could drive it programmatically. Their published stack makes the separation visible as distinct products: Agent OS as the runtime, Agent Studio as the authoring surface, Ghostwriter as the generative layer on top. The interesting engineering question, for either vendor or for a platform you build yourself, is whether the specs the generative layer produces are inspectable, versionable, and portable across surfaces — because that is what separates a platform from a magic box.

The key claim is that all four surfaces should produce the same AgentSpec, consumed by the same runtime. If they don't, you have four disconnected products pretending to be one platform. This is exactly the mistake most "agent builder" vendors have baked into their architecture, which is why migrating off them is so painful.

The AgentSpec is a plain data structure. It references tools by name (resolved against the tool registry), references memory scopes by identifier, declares its model preferences, carries its system prompt, and optionally names an orchestration topology. Roughly:

AgentSpec:
  id:              string
  name:            string
  system_prompt:   string
  model:           ModelPreference
  tools:           string[]           // resolved against Tool registry
  memory_scopes:   string[]           // resolved against Memory layer
  context_policy:  ContextPolicy      // budgets, compression
  orchestration:   Orchestration?     // null for single-agent
  guardrails:      Guardrails
  metadata:        { ... }

That spec is what every builder produces and what the runtime consumes. The builder becomes interchangeable. The platform becomes the thing that actually matters.

The reframe

The current market confuses authoring surfaces with platforms. A company that buys an “agent builder” is usually buying layer 7 attached to someone else’s layers 1 through 6 — and inheriting that vendor’s choices about context, memory, tools, orchestration, and observability, often without realizing those choices were made.

Building your own agentic platform means owning layers 1 through 6 and being explicit about which authoring surfaces sit on top. For some teams that is overreach; an off-the-shelf platform — Wonderful, Sierra, or any of the other vertical and horizontal platforms that will consolidate in the next two years — is the right call. For teams whose agents become core to the product, or whose regulatory and operational context resists packaged solutions, owning the layers is how you retain the ability to change your mind. At BRQ, where we operate our own platform (Fusion), these are weekly decisions, not abstractions: what to build, what to buy, and what to own explicitly because owning it is the product.

The agent builder, whatever it ends up looking like, is the last thing you build — not the first. If the layers below it are honest, multiple builders can sit on top without fighting each other. If the layers below it are muddled, no builder rescues the platform. The map is the precondition. The builder is the consequence.

Daniel Braz is CTO at BRQ Product & Experience Studios, where he leads digital product engineering and applied AI research. He writes about specification-driven development, agentic workflows, and the evolving role of human judgment in AI-assisted software engineering.

The anatomy of an agentic platform was originally published in CodeToDeploy on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: Agents Didn’t Repeal the Laws of Software Engineering. They Intensified Them.
items.description:
On the 13 laws that circulate in every LinkedIn post — and what they reveal when there’s an agent in the loop.

It showed up on my feed again — that list of “13 software engineering laws.” Parkinson, Brooks, Conway, and friends. It’s a genre I’ve seen fifty times: posts celebrating the folklore of the discipline, each law with a little one-line gloss, a vaguely nostalgic tone.

But looking at that list in 2026, with code agents running inside nearly every serious engineering team, is a strange experience. Not because the laws feel obsolete — the opposite. Because they feel more true. Many of these aphorisms, coined to describe human teams writing code by hand, now describe with uncomfortable precision what happens when autonomous agents enter the loop.

The thesis of this post is simple: agents didn’t repeal the laws. They intensified them. And walking through the thirteen one by one, with that lens, reveals something about where AI-assisted software engineering is heading — and where it’s most likely to fall on its face.

1. Parkinson — work expands to fill the context window

Original: work expands to fill the time available.

Current version: work expands to fill the context window. Hand an agent 200k tokens and a simple task, and you get 200k tokens of “solution” — unnecessary refactors, redundant tests, bloated documentation, speculative architecture. The context ceiling isn’t a limit; it’s a discipline. Without it, the agent over-engineers by default.

Practical corollary: specifying what not to do has become as important as specifying what to do.

2. Brooks — adding agents to a late project makes it later

The hype cycle of the moment is “AI squads” — multi-agent orchestration with clean role definitions (planner, coder, reviewer, tester). The implicit promise is linear parallelization.

Brooks answered this in 1975: coordination cost grows non-linearly with the number of participants. In multi-agent systems, that cost shows up as context fragmentation, bad handoffs, loss of shared theory (Naur, 1985). Every new agent in the pipeline is one more boundary where the theory of the problem has to be reconstituted — and reconstituting theory is precisely what agents are bad at.

In practice, one deep agent with carefully curated context beats five shallow ones in a pretty pipeline.

3. Conway — your agent architecture mirrors your team

Conway captured in 1968 that system design reflects communication structure. In human-agent teams, the law operates at two levels: the final system reflects communication between the agents, and the architecture of the agents reflects how the human team thinks about the problem.

If your team organizes work as “frontend / backend / infra,” your agents will end up that way. If it organizes around domains (DDD), the agents will mirror domains. The prompt is an organizational artifact before it’s a technical one — and the decision of how many agents you orchestrate is, first and foremost, a decision about how your team communicates.

4. Cunningham — the best way to get the right answer from an LLM is to propose a wrong one

Cunningham’s original observation was that the best way to get a correct answer on the internet is to post an incorrect one — someone will show up to correct you. LLMs inherit this almost literally: a bad draft is a better prompt than an open question.

“Write an auth service” produces generic slop. “Here’s my bad version, critique and rewrite it” produces decent work. Planner/critic patterns, adversarial review, self-consistency — all of them are Cunningham operationalized. The agent works better attacking something than creating from scratch.

5. Zawinski — every agent expands until it can read email

Zawinski observed that every program expands until it can read mail. The modern version: every agent expands until it can call every MCP server on your network.

Start with a code review agent. Three months in, it needs Jira access. Six months in, Slack. Nine months in, Gmail, “to better contextualize.” Feature creep has become tool creep — and the attack surface grows with it. Every new tool is a new way for the agent to make decisions nobody explicitly asked for.

6. Hyrum — every emergent model behavior will be depended on by someone

Hyrum’s law: with enough users, every observable behavior of an API will be depended on by someone — including the unspecified ones.

LLMs are the extreme materialization of this. Your prompt depends on a specific way the model formats lists. Your agent framework depends on a tool-call pattern the model never promised to keep. Upgrade from Sonnet 4.6 to 4.7, and half your production prompts need review. “Model upgrade” has become a synonym for “silent regression across the pipeline.”

Every prompt in production carries an implicit debt to Hyrum’s law.

7. Price — half the value comes from the square root of your agents

Price generalized the 80/20 rule: half the work comes from the square root of the people. In any sufficiently large ecosystem, the long tail contributes little.

Look at the MCP ecosystem today: hundreds of servers listed, a handful actually used. Look at your own setup: if you have ten agents configured, three of them probably deliver half the value. The myth of massive orchestration runs into the same empirical fact Price described in human organizations — value concentrates, it doesn’t distribute. Building ten mediocre agents doesn’t add up to one good one.

8. Ringelmann — per-agent productivity drops as the swarm grows

The Ringelmann effect: individual productivity drops as group size grows. In humans, it’s social loafing. In agents, it’s context fragmentation. The more agents you chain, the smaller the fraction of useful context each one carries, the more handoff noise, the lower the quality of each step.

One agent with the whole context beats an orchestra of agents with partial contexts — unless the problem is genuinely decomposable, which is rarer than the multi-agent literature suggests.

9. Goodhart — when a benchmark becomes a target, it stops measuring capability

Probably the most obvious and most ignored law of the moment. SWE-bench, HumanEval, MMLU — all of them became targets, all of them saturated. Not because models got genuinely better at programming, but because the community optimized for those specific metrics.

Practical corollary for anyone building with agents: your internal evals need to be distinct from the public ones, and they need to run on a distribution that represents your domain. Otherwise you’re measuring how good the model is at lookinggood — not at being good.

10. Gilb — anything worth doing is worth measuring, agents included

Gilb is the necessary counterweight to Goodhart: even a crude metric beats no metric at all. In agentic engineering, this advice is almost universally ignored. Most teams run agents in production with no structured evals — just vibes.

The discipline is clear: measure without turning it into an OKR, iterate on evals with the same care as on features, treat agent metrics like any other system metric — noisy, imperfect, indispensable. The question “did the agent get better or worse after this change?” needs a quantitative answer, not a gut feeling.

11. Murphy — in non-deterministic systems, everything will go wrong

Murphy was a joke when systems were deterministic. With LLMs, it’s a technical specification. If there’s an input that makes the agent take a catastrophic decision, and you have production volume, that input will eventually arrive.

Engineering stops being about the happy path and becomes about the distribution of paths. Guardrails, fallbacks, human confirmation on irreversible actions, dry-runs by default — all of this is Murphy engineered, not paranoia. Anyone designing for the happy path in a probabilistic system is accumulating operational debt without noticing.

12. Hofstadter — it always takes longer, agents included

METR published a 2025 empirical study showing that experienced open-source developers using AI agents were, on average, slower — despite reporting they felt faster. The Copilot study (Peng et al.) pointed the same direction in specific subgroups.

Hofstadter generalized the phenomenon decades ago: it always takes longer than you expect, even when you account for Hofstadter’s law. Agents add a new layer: perceived velocity accelerates before delivered velocity does. Demos are fast; production is slow; the gap between the two is where deadlines die.

13. Sturgeon — 90% of everything is slop, agent output included

Sturgeon said 90% of everything is crap. The current generation of agents isn’t an exception — it’s a demonstration.

90% of agent-generated code needs review, refactoring, or outright deletion. 90% of MCP servers shouldn’t exist. 90% of “AI-first” products are wrappers that will disappear in the next cycle. This isn’t a criticism of the technology; it’s a description of the normal state of any new ecosystem. The engineering question is: how do you organize your flow so that the useful 10% makes it to production, and the 90% slop is discarded before it does damage?

What the list actually reveals

Look at the thirteen again. How many are laws about code? Two — Hyrum and Zawinski. The rest are about humans, organizations, communication, attention, measurement, failure.

This isn’t accidental. It’s diagnostic. The discipline we call “software engineering” is overwhelmingly sociotechnical — and its folk aphorisms registered this long before we had the academic vocabulary to describe it (Naur, Polanyi, Suchman).

Agents don’t change the nature of the problem. They change the scale, the speed, and the failure surface. The laws keep holding because they describe invariants — structural constraints on how humans, organizations, and complex systems interact. Swapping a “junior programmer” for a “code agent” doesn’t erase Brooks, doesn’t repeal Conway, doesn’t suspend Goodhart.

The mistake of today’s agentic-engineering culture is treating these laws as problems to be routed around by better technology — as if a sufficiently sophisticated multi-agent framework nullifies Brooksian coordination cost, or a sufficiently clever eval neutralizes Goodhart. It doesn’t nullify. It doesn’t neutralize. Technology redistributes where the cost shows up; it doesn’t eliminate it.

The honest engineering stance is the opposite: treat these laws as boundary conditions. Design the system — humans and agents together — knowing that Brooks will charge, Conway will model, Hyrum will break, Murphy will happen. Teams who do this ship. Teams who believe agents are an exception to the history of the discipline don’t.

The folklore always knew more than the manuals. It still does.

Daniel Braz is a CTO and practitioner-researcher focused on agentic software engineering, spec-driven development, and AI-assisted workflows. He writes about the engineering realities behind the AI hype.

Agents Didn’t Repeal the Laws of Software Engineering. They Intensified Them. was originally published in Level Up Coding on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: Building a daily ops agent with LangSmith Fleet: an architecture case study
items.description:
From no-code prompt to production agent — and every architectural decision in between.

In a previous article, I mapped the LangChain ecosystem: what each piece does, when to use it, when not to. This article is the concrete follow-up. We’re going to build something real — a Daily Ops Agent — and I’ll show you exactly where each ecosystem component earns its place, where the no-code path hits its limits, and what the observability layer actually reveals once the agent is running.

The goal isn’t a tutorial you copy-paste. It’s an architecture walkthrough where every decision is visible: why Fleet here, why the SDK there, what LangSmith traces told us that the agent’s output alone couldn’t.

The problem: morning ops is a tax

If you lead a team, your first hour every morning looks something like this: scan your calendar for today’s meetings, skim email for anything urgent, check Slack for overnight threads that need attention, and mentally assemble a picture of “what matters today.” It’s 30–45 minutes of context assembly before you do any actual work.

This is a perfect agent use case: structured enough that the task is well-defined, repetitive enough that automation pays off, and low-risk enough that an imperfect first version is still useful.

Here’s what we want our Daily Ops Agent to do:

  1. Morning briefing — Scan today’s calendar, flag meetings that need prep, summarize overnight emails and Slack threads.
  2. Email triage — Categorize incoming emails as urgent / needs reply / FYI / ignorable. Draft replies for the “needs reply” bucket.
  3. Human-in-the-loop — Never send a reply without approval. Flag anything ambiguous for manual review.
  4. Delivery — Post the briefing to a dedicated Slack channel every morning at 8:00 AM.

Now let’s build it.

Phase 1: starting with Fleet (no-code)

The fastest path from zero to a working agent in the LangChain ecosystem is LangSmith Fleet. You describe what you want in natural language, connect your tools, and Fleet generates the agent configuration — including the system prompt, tool bindings, and trigger setup.

What we did

In Fleet’s chat interface, we described the agent:

“Create a daily ops agent that runs at 8:00 AM São Paulo time. It should check my Google Calendar for today’s events, scan my Gmail for unread messages from the last 12 hours, and check Slack for unread mentions. Produce a morning briefing organized by priority. For emails that need a reply, draft a response but don’t send it — ask me first.”

Fleet did several things automatically:

  • Generated a system prompt with detailed instructions for prioritization logic, output formatting, and the human-in-the-loop constraint.
  • Selected built-in tools: Google Calendar, Gmail, and Slack (all available as first-party integrations via OAuth).
  • Configured a Schedule trigger for daily execution at 11:00 UTC (8:00 AM BRT).
  • Set up a Slack Channel as the delivery mechanism — the agent posts its briefing to a designated channel.

The architecture at this point

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                LangSmith Fleet              │
│                                             │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌──────────────┐  │
│  │ Schedule │→ │ Agent │→ │ Slack Output │  │
│  │ 11:00UTC │  │       │  │   #daily-ops │  │
│  └──────────┘  │       │  └──────────────┘  │
│                │       │                    │
│        ┌───────┴───────┴───────┐            │
│        │        Tools          │            │
│        │ Calendar │ Gmail │ Slack│          │
│        └───────────────────────┘            │
│                                             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐│
│  │  LangSmith Tracing (automatic)          ││
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘│
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Every Fleet agent gets LangSmith tracing for free. Every tool call, every LLM decision, every output — captured in a structured trace. This isn’t something you configure. It’s built in.

What worked

The agent produced a usable morning briefing on day one. Calendar events were correctly parsed. Emails were categorized with reasonable accuracy. The Slack delivery was reliable. For a zero-code setup, this was genuinely impressive — maybe 20 minutes from description to first run.

Where it broke

Three issues surfaced within the first week:

1. Email triage quality was inconsistent. The agent couldn’t distinguish between “urgent from my CEO” and “urgent from a vendor’s marketing automation.” Priority requires context that a flat inbox doesn’t provide — you need sender history, thread patterns, and organizational hierarchy.

2. Draft replies were too generic. Fleet agents use a single system prompt. There was no way to inject per-sender context (“when replying to this person, use Portuguese” or “this client prefers formal tone”).

3. The 8 AM schedule was too rigid. Some mornings I needed the briefing at 7:00, others at 9:00. Fleet schedules are fixed cron expressions — there’s no “trigger when I open Slack” option.

This is the architectural decision point: when does the no-code path stop being sufficient?

Phase 2: dropping to the SDK for custom logic

Fleet agents are, under the hood, Deep Agents running on a LangSmith Deployment. Fleet’s “call from code” feature lets you invoke any Fleet agent via the LangGraph SDK. This means you don’t have to rebuild from scratch — you can keep Fleet for the parts it handles well and add custom orchestration around it.

Decision: hybrid architecture

Instead of abandoning Fleet entirely, we split the architecture:

  • Fleet owns: tool connections (OAuth for Calendar, Gmail, Slack), scheduling, delivery, and the base agent identity/permissions.
  • Custom SDK layer owns: email triage logic, reply drafting with per-sender context, and the human-approval workflow.

Here’s the key code decision — wrapping the Fleet agent in a LangGraph workflow:

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langsmith import Client

client = Client()

# Fleet agent invocation via LangGraph SDK
async def fetch_raw_briefing(state):
    """Call the Fleet agent to gather raw data from Calendar, Gmail, Slack."""
    response = await client.runs.create(
        assistant_id="daily-ops-fleet-agent",
        input={"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Run morning scan"}]},
    )
    state["raw_briefing"] = response["output"]
    return state

async def enrich_email_triage(state):
    """Apply custom triage logic with sender context."""
    emails = state["raw_briefing"]["emails"]
    
    for email in emails:
        sender = email["from"]
        # Look up sender context from our internal knowledge base
        sender_profile = await get_sender_profile(sender)
        email["priority_score"] = calculate_priority(
            email, sender_profile, state["user_preferences"]
        )
        email["reply_language"] = sender_profile.get("preferred_language", "en")
        email["reply_tone"] = sender_profile.get("tone", "professional")
    
    state["triaged_emails"] = sorted(emails, key=lambda e: e["priority_score"], reverse=True)
    return state

async def draft_replies(state):
    """Generate context-aware reply drafts for high-priority emails."""
    drafts = []
    for email in state["triaged_emails"]:
        if email["priority_score"] >= REPLY_THRESHOLD:
            draft = await generate_reply(
                email=email,
                language=email["reply_language"],
                tone=email["reply_tone"],
                context=email.get("thread_history", []),
            )
            drafts.append({"email": email, "draft": draft, "approved": False})
    
    state["pending_drafts"] = drafts
    return state

async def request_approval(state):
    """Post drafts for human review. Block until approved."""
    # This uses LangGraph's interrupt mechanism
    # The workflow pauses here until the human responds
    return state  # LangGraph handles the interrupt

# Build the graph
workflow = StateGraph(OpsAgentState)
workflow.add_node("fetch", fetch_raw_briefing)
workflow.add_node("triage", enrich_email_triage)
workflow.add_node("draft", draft_replies)
workflow.add_node("approve", request_approval)
workflow.add_node("deliver", deliver_briefing)

workflow.set_entry_point("fetch")

workflow.add_edge("fetch", "triage")
workflow.add_edge("triage", "draft")
workflow.add_edge("draft", "approve")
workflow.add_edge("approve", "deliver")
workflow.add_edge("deliver", END)

Why this shape?

A few architectural decisions worth calling out:

Fleet as data gatherer, LangGraph as orchestrator. Fleet handles OAuth, tool connections, and the basic scan — things that are painful to set up from scratch and that Fleet does well. The custom layer handles the logic that requires domain context. This avoids rebuilding OAuth flows while gaining full control over triage and drafting.

LangGraph’s interrupt for human-in-the-loop. The request_approval node uses LangGraph's native interrupt mechanism. The workflow literally pauses — state is checkpointed — and resumes only when the human approves, edits, or rejects each draft. This is fundamentally different from Fleet's built-in HITL, which operates at the tool-call level (approve/reject individual tool executions). We need approval at the output level — "is this draft good enough to send?" — which requires the graph-level interrupt.

Sender profiles as external context. The triage quality jumped significantly once we added sender profiles. This is a simple lookup table (who is this person, what’s their relationship to me, what’s their communication preference) but it’s the kind of context that a generic agent can’t infer from a single email. The lesson: agent quality often depends more on the context you inject than on the model you choose.

Phase 3: LangSmith observability — what the traces revealed

Here’s where things get interesting. With LangSmith tracing active on both the Fleet agent and the custom LangGraph workflow, we had full visibility into every step. Not just “did it work?” but “why did it make that decision?”

Trace anatomy

A single morning run produces a trace with roughly this structure:

Run: daily-ops-2026-04-01T11:00:00Z
├── fetch_raw_briefing
│   └── Fleet Agent Run
│       ├── LLM Call: plan morning scan
│       ├── Tool: google_calendar.list_events
│       ├── Tool: gmail.search_messages  
│       ├── Tool: slack.conversations_history (x3 channels)
│       └── LLM Call: synthesize raw briefing
├── enrich_email_triage
│   ├── get_sender_profile (x12 emails)
│   └── calculate_priority (x12 emails)
├── draft_replies
│   ├── LLM Call: draft reply for [CEO email] 
│   ├── LLM Call: draft reply for [client email]
│   └── LLM Call: draft reply for [partner email]
├── request_approval
│   └── [INTERRUPTED — waiting for human]
│   └── [RESUMED — 3 approved, 0 rejected]
└── deliver_briefing
    └── Tool: slack.chat_postMessage

Three things the traces taught us

1. The Gmail search was the bottleneck.

Trace latency breakdown showed that gmail.search_messages was consuming 40% of total run time — not because Gmail's API is slow, but because the Fleet agent was making three separate search calls (inbox, starred, sent) instead of one consolidated query. We couldn't fix this inside Fleet (the search strategy is part of the generated system prompt), but we could see it clearly in the trace. The fix was to override the email collection in our custom fetch_raw_briefing node with a single targeted Gmail API call.

2. The triage model was miscategorizing internal newsletters.

LangSmith’s evaluation feature let us build a dataset of 50 categorized emails and run the triage logic against it. Results: 92% accuracy on external emails, but only 68% on internal company emails — specifically, internal newsletters were being flagged as “urgent” because they contained keywords like “deadline” and “action required.” The fix was a simple rule: if sender domain matches the company domain AND the email is sent to a distribution list, cap priority at “FYI.” No model change needed — just context.

3. Reply drafts improved 30% after we added thread history.

We A/B tested drafts with and without thread history (the previous messages in the email conversation) using LangSmith’s pairwise annotation queues. Two team members blindly rated 40 draft pairs. Drafts with thread context were preferred 73% of the time. The cost was minimal — one extra Gmail API call per thread — but the quality improvement was measurable. Without LangSmith’s evaluation tooling, this would have been a gut-feel decision.

The final architecture

After three weeks of iteration, here’s where we landed:

                    ┌──────────────┐
                    │   Schedule   │
                    │ (Fleet/cron) │
                    └──────┬───────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │  LangGraph   │
                    │  Orchestrator│
                    └──────┬───────┘
                           │
              ┌────────────┼────────────┐
              │            │            │
       ┌──────▼───────┐ ┌──▼───┐ ┌──────▼──────┐
       │ Fleet Agent  │ │Custom│ │   Custom    │
       │ (data scan)  │ │Triage│ │   Drafter   │
       │              │ │      │ │             │
       │ • Calendar   │ │Sender│ │ • Thread    │
       │ • Gmail      │ │profiles│ │  history  │
       │ • Slack      │ │+ rules│ │ • Tone/lang│
       └──────────────┘ └──────┘ └─────────────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │   Human      │
                    │  Approval    │
                    │  (interrupt) │
                    └──────┬───────┘
                           │
                    ┌──────▼───────┐
                    │ Slack Output │
                    │  #daily-ops  │
                    └──────────────┘
        ═══════════════════════════════════
        │     LangSmith (full tracing)    │
        │  traces · evals · cost tracking │
        ═══════════════════════════════════

Cost and performance

After optimization, one daily run looks like this:

Metric Value Total latency ~45 seconds LLM calls 6–8 (depending on email volume) Token cost ~$0.03–0.05 per run Human review time 2–3 minutes Time saved vs. manual ~30 minutes/day

At roughly $1.50/month in API costs, the ROI is absurd. But cost isn’t the interesting part — the interesting part is what we learned about building hybrid agent architectures.

Lessons: what actually matters

1. Start no-code, graduate to code at the seams

Fleet got us to a working agent in 20 minutes. The custom SDK layer took two days. If we’d started with the SDK, it would have taken a week — most of that spent on OAuth flows and tool connections that Fleet handles for free. The pattern is: let Fleet own the integrations, own the orchestration yourself.

2. Observability isn’t optional — it’s the feedback loop

Without LangSmith traces, we would have shipped the agent on day one and wondered why the triage quality felt “meh.” The traces turned vague dissatisfaction into specific, fixable problems: a redundant Gmail search, a missing sender context lookup, a thread history gap. Every improvement we made came from reading traces, not from guessing.

3. Human-in-the-loop is an architectural choice, not a feature flag

Fleet’s built-in HITL works at the tool level — “should I call this tool?” That’s useful for preventing destructive actions, but it’s not the same as “is this output good enough?” LangGraph’s interrupt mechanism gives you output-level control: the workflow pauses, the human reviews the actual artifact (the draft reply), and the workflow resumes. If your agent produces artifacts that go to external humans (emails, messages, documents), you need output-level approval. Plan for it architecturally.

4. Agent quality is mostly a context engineering problem

The biggest quality improvements came from injecting better context (sender profiles, thread history, priority rules), not from model upgrades or prompt engineering. This aligns with a broader pattern I’ve been seeing across agentic systems: the model provides the reasoning, but the context determines the quality. Invest your time in what the agent knows, not just how it thinks.

5. The ecosystem layers compose well — when you respect the boundaries

Fleet for integrations and governance. LangGraph for orchestration and control flow. LangSmith for observability and evaluation. Each layer has a clear purpose, and the boundaries are clean enough to build hybrid architectures. The mistake would be trying to force Fleet to do custom orchestration, or trying to build OAuth flows in raw LangGraph. Use each piece for what it’s good at.

When this architecture doesn’t fit

To be fair about the limits:

  • If your productivity needs are simple (just a calendar summary), Fleet alone is sufficient. The hybrid architecture adds complexity that only pays off when you need custom logic.
  • If you’re not in the LangChain ecosystem, this approach locks you in. The Fleet → SDK → LangSmith pipeline is tightly integrated by design.
  • If latency matters (sub-second responses), the multi-step pipeline adds overhead. This architecture assumes batch/scheduled execution, not real-time chat.
  • If you’re a team of one, Fleet’s governance features (permissions, agent identity, audit trails) are overhead you don’t need.

What’s next

Two directions I’m exploring:

Memory across runs. Currently, each morning run is independent. Fleet agents have built-in memory (file-based, persisted across sessions), but our custom LangGraph layer doesn’t leverage it yet. The goal: the agent should learn that “emails from this sender on Mondays are always FYI” without me writing explicit rules.

Multi-agent expansion. The Daily Ops Agent handles the morning. But what about the “End of Day Agent” that summarizes what got done, flags unresolved threads, and prepares tomorrow’s agenda? Fleet’s workspace model supports multiple agents with shared tools and permissions — the “fleet” in the name starts to make literal sense.

If you built something similar or hit different walls, I’d like to hear about it. The interesting problems in agentic systems right now aren’t about the models — they’re about the architectures we build around them.

Daniel Braz is a CTO and practitioner-researcher focused on agentic software engineering, spec-driven development, and AI-assisted workflows.

Building a daily ops agent with LangSmith Fleet: an architecture case study was originally published in Level Up Coding on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: A confissão das labs
items.description:
Em duas semanas de maio, OpenAI e Anthropic admitiram, na mesma estrutura societária, o que vinha sendo cuidadosamente evitado em decks de investidor: o modelo não é mais o gargalo. A implementação é.

A Anthropic anunciou uma joint venture de US$ 1,5 bilhão com Blackstone, Hellman & Friedman e Goldman Sachs. A OpenAI estruturou a OpenAI Deployment Co com US$ 4 bilhões liderados por TPG, e adquiriu a Tomoro para começar com 150 engenheiros. Ambas copiam o modelo do Palantir: forward-deployed engineer, embarcado no cliente, ficando até a coisa virar produção.

A confissão é honesta. Com valuation de US$ 850 bilhões, dentro de uma estrutura societária que pressupõe Blackstone como canal. Para o mercado americano, é um veículo bem desenhado e provavelmente vencedor. Para o mercado brasileiro e latino-americano, é uma notícia diferente — e bem melhor para quem está aqui.

Provavelmente as labs vencem nos EUA

O modelo que OpenAI e Anthropic acabaram de montar é desenhado para o mercado americano e tem boa probabilidade de funcionar lá.

Os ingredientes estão alinhados. O canal de private equity é poderoso — Blackstone tem centenas de portfolio companies que viram pipeline para a JV da Anthropic, TPG tem o mesmo para a OpenAI Deployment Co. A economia do forward-deployed engineer fecha quando o cliente é uma empresa americana de US$ 5 bilhões de receita que paga US$ 2 milhões por uma implementação de doze semanas: o engenheiro embarcado de US$ 400 mil/ano cabe nessa equação.

O modelo de serviço é familiar para o comprador — o C-level americano já comprou Palantir, já comprou McKinsey em parceria com SaaS, já entende o que significa “embedded team.” E o ciclo de feedback entre engenheiro embarcado e roadmap do modelo é um moat real, que se compõe ao longo de meses.

A previsão honesta: em 18 a 24 meses, OpenAI Deployment Co e a JV da Anthropic vão ter capturado uma fatia significativa do gasto de implementação de IA nas mid-to-upper Fortune 1000 nos EUA. As consultorias americanas que tentarem competir frontalmente — querer ser a “OpenAI Deployment Co alternativa” — vão perder. As que viraram parceiras competentes no ecossistema vão capturar uma fatia menor, porém defensável, do trabalho.

Esse é o cenário razoável para o mercado americano. Ele não se transfere para a América Latina.

Por que o modelo não atravessa

A confissão das labs tem um problema embutido para quem está fora dos EUA. O veículo de US$ 5,5 bilhões foi calibrado para uma economia, um canal, uma regulação, um idioma e um modelo de compra específicos. Cada um desses cinco eixos quebra quando você atravessa o equador.

Economia. OpenAI e Anthropic podem operar com engenheiros embarcados em São Paulo a custo local — Globant e EPAM fazem isso há vinte anos, e nada impede uma operação brasileira das labs de calibrar folha à realidade daqui. A questão real é outra: alocação de capital. O bottleneck atual das duas companhias é capacidade no mercado americano, onde o ticket médio por implementação é 3 a 5 vezes maior e onde o ciclo de feedback do engenheiro embarcado para o roadmap do modelo é estrategicamente mais valioso do que o equivalente brasileiro seria. Cada engenheiro sênior alocado em ramp de operação BR é um engenheiro não capturando conta da Fortune 1000. A lógica favorece foco no mercado americano pelos próximos 24 meses — e ramp relevante em mercado novo leva exatamente esse tempo.

Canal. Em PE no Brasil, mid-market é dominado por gestoras locais que não compõem o canal das JVs americanas. As portfolio companies da Blackstone no Brasil são poucas, em geral muito grandes, e tomam decisões de tecnologia de forma idiossincrática. A venda via portfolio companies das parceiras — o motor central das duas JVs — não tem volume relevante aqui. A venda direta da OpenAI Deployment Co a uma empresa brasileira média vai esbarrar em ciclos de 9 a 18 meses, sem o relacionamento prévio que faz o motor americano girar.

Regulação. LGPD não é GDPR com tradução. Regulação setorial é específica — Resolução 4658 do Banco Central para instituições financeiras, normas da ANS para saúde, exigências da ANATEL em telecom. Requisitos de residência de dados, transferência internacional e tratamento de dados pessoais sensíveis variam por setor de forma que não é parametrizável a partir de um template americano. Implementar agente que toca dados regulados no Brasil exige conhecimento que não está em compliance training corporativo.

Idioma e domínio. Não se trata de português brasileiro versus inglês americano. Trata-se de saber que boleto é uma via com regras próprias, que nota fiscal eletrônica é integração regulatória mandatória, que PIX mudou a economia de payment companies em três anos, que CNPJ tem dígitos verificadores com semântica que sistemas legados não respeitam. Um agente que responde cliente de banco brasileiro precisa entender hierarquia de produtos que não tem equivalente americano. Isso não se localiza com fine-tuning. Localiza-se com engenheiro que entende.

Modelo de compra. A jornada de compra empresarial no Brasil tem cadência diferente. A relação importa mais. Procurement é mais formal e mais lento. Implementação em “embedded team” pressupõe um nível de abertura organizacional que muitas empresas brasileiras grandes ainda não têm — o time interno precisa ser convencido antes de qualquer terceiro entrar. Não é resistência irracional, é uma forma diferente de gerenciar risco político interno. Quem vende implementação de IA aqui precisa entender essa coreografia.

Nenhum desses cinco eixos é intransponível. Mas o veículo anunciado em maio não foi desenhado para resolver nenhum deles. Foi desenhado para um mercado onde eles não são problemas.

O que “localizar” de fato significa

Quando a OpenAI Deployment Co eventualmente abrir operação em São Paulo — e vai abrir, provavelmente em 12 a 18 meses, provavelmente em parceria com algum SI grande — a tentação será descrever isso como “expansão internacional.” Não é. É refazer o modelo do zero.

Localizar de verdade exige cinco mudanças simultâneas, cada uma com economia própria. Mudar a economia do engenheiro embarcado para algo que feche em ticket brasileiro. Construir canal sem depender de PE portfolio. Internalizar regulação setorial vertical por vertical. Construir capacidade de domínio em português com pessoas que já viveram aqui. E adaptar o modelo de atendimento para uma jornada de compra mais relacional e mais política.

Cada uma dessas mudanças é caríssima. Em conjunto, exigem reconstruir o veículo. E o tempo que leva para fazer isso é o tempo que consultorias regionais têm para construir capacidade e capturar o mercado.

A oportunidade estratégica

A oportunidade é específica. Existe uma janela de 24 a 36 meses durante a qual o mid-market brasileiro e latino-americano vai precisar implementar IA empresarial — e durante a qual nem a OpenAI Deployment Co nem a JV da Anthropic vão estar operando aqui na forma que operam nos EUA. Esse mercado, por estrutura, pertence a consultorias regionais que já estão dentro desses clientes, já entendem regulação setorial e já operam em real.

A pergunta estratégica não é se consultorias regionais vão capturar esse espaço. É quais chegam com capacidade construída — e quais chegam como revendedoras de API americana, com margem comprimida no tempo.

Três movimentos concretos separam as duas posições.

Capacidade proprietária de harness engineering, neutra em relação ao modelo. Integrada com os fluxos regulados brasileiros, capaz de operar com modelos open-weights self-hosted quando o caso de uso exigir — o que vai ser comum em FIs e saúde. Não é tecnologia que se compra; é tecnologia que se constrói, em ciclo curto, com engenheiros que já estão entregando.

Profundidade vertical. Vai ganhar quem souber, em detalhe, como uma seguradora brasileira processa sinistro, como um banco médio brasileiro estrutura crédito consignado, como um varejo brasileiro lida com SKU em múltiplos estados com ICMS diferente. Esse conhecimento está distribuído em pessoas que trabalharam na indústria, e capturá-lo dentro de uma consultoria exige decisão deliberada de contratar e organizar diferente.

Posição de partner preferencial. OpenAI e Anthropic vão precisar de partners de implementação no Brasil — não por estratégia de canal, por necessidade econômica. A pergunta não é se vão precisar, é quem vão escolher. A resposta vai favorecer quem chegar antes com capacidade demonstrada, propriedade intelectual visível e clientes em produção.

A confissão lida do sul

OpenAI e Anthropic admitiram publicamente, com US$ 5,5 bilhões em capital, que implementação é o moat. Esse é o sinal mais claro possível, vindo das próprias labs, de que o valor está se deslocando para a camada onde consultorias têm vantagem estrutural.

No mercado americano, as labs provavelmente vencem essa fatia — o veículo que montaram foi desenhado para vencer lá. No mercado brasileiro e latino-americano, a estrutura é diferente. Economia, canal, regulação, idioma e modelo de atendimento criam barreiras de localização que as labs vão demorar a vencer. Essa demora é a janela. Ela está aberta agora, e vai ficar aberta entre dois e três anos.

A confissão das labs é uma boa notícia. Mas ela foi dita em inglês, em valuation de US$ 850 bilhões, dentro de uma estrutura societária que pressupõe Blackstone como canal. Para ser uma boa notícia em português, ela precisa ser localizada — e quem localiza é quem captura.

Daniel Braz is CTO at BRQ Product & Experience Studios, where he leads a digital products studio focused on AI-augmented software engineering. He is currently researching context degradation and specification-driven development in agentic workflows.

items.title: The wider harness
items.description:
Harness engineering gave us a better name for the work around the model. Building digital workers asks the harness to stretch one layer further.

The phrase harness engineering has done a quiet but useful job in the last eighteen months. It gave us a better name for the work most teams were still calling prompt engineering.

The shorthand carries Harrison Chase’s argument about Deep Agents, Anthropic’s published reflections on Claude Code, and how platforms like Wonderful position themselves around skills and continuous evaluation. What used to be write a better prompt has become engineer the scaffolding around the model: planning tools, subagent isolation, filesystem as scratchpad, context discipline, evaluation running in production. The model is one component. The harness is the system.

The discipline is real, and the architectural promise is being delivered. But the harness that has been formalised so far is the harness for a task agent — an agent given a discrete piece of work, expected to complete it inside one or a few sessions, then move on. The Claude Code harness exists so a model writes code well across a few hours. The Deep Agents harness exists so a research agent doesn’t unravel across thirty tool calls. A customer-service harness exists so a skill stays robust across deploys.

What some teams are starting to build in production is a different unit of work entirely. Not a task agent operating a workflow, but a digital worker operating a function. The difference is not size. It is horizon.

The unit shift

A task agent is contracted for an output. A digital worker is contracted for an outcome that recurs.

You don’t deploy that. You onboard it.

The vocabulary shift is not cosmetic. The system prompt does the work of identity — until two analysts disagree about who owns the worker. The toolset does the work of capability — until an auditor needs to know what the worker is permitted to touch. The memory store does the work of cognition — until a thesis is updated and you need to reconstruct which historical decisions were made under which version.
