The logs do the work of governance — until something goes wrong and who do I call has no single answer.

None of these are model problems. They are harness problems, but they sit at a layer above the loop.

Six dimensions of the wider harness

The shape the wider harness lands on is not an invention. It is the set of questions any organisation asks before a hire: who are you, what do you need to know, what are you able to do, how do you behave, how do you learn, who owns the result.

Identity answers who is this? For a task agent, identity collapses into a system prompt. For a digital worker, identity is a contract: a name, a purpose stated in one sentence, an explicit scope, an explicit non-scope, and a single human sponsor who owns the function the worker now operates beside. Tracking identity at this resolution is what makes a worker hireable — and, when needed, retireable.

Context answers what does it need to know to operate? In task-agent harnesses this is essentially the prompt plus a retrieval system. In a digital worker, context is inherited from the function: the active strategy of the area that contracts the worker, the brand voice it speaks in, the named humans it relates to on each side, the environment of systems it touches. Strategy is inherited from the area-client, not invented by the engineering team. That separation protects the function from becoming a software project and the studio from becoming a strategy department.

Capability is the closest the wider harness gets to the existing one. Skills, tools, workflows, deliverables — the layer where most of the harness-engineering literature already lives. The discipline transfers cleanly: composable units, governed independently, validated in isolation, propagating fixes through composition rather than through prompt edits. Where the wider frame adds something is in capability boundaries. A digital worker has named capabilities, each with a defined trigger, a defined output, a measurable acceptance criterion. The capability is not what the worker can do. It is what it is accountable for.

Conduct answers how does it act? This is where autonomy lives, and where the task-agent harness is least equipped. In a task agent, autonomy is a configuration: tools either get called or they don’t, approvals either happen or they don’t. In a digital worker, autonomy is a gradient — granular per action type and progressed on measured maturity. Detecting an announced funding round in European press is reversible and low-risk; it can run autonomously within weeks. Dispatching outreach to a founder is irreversible; it stays in explicit human approval indefinitely. Conduct is also where the worker’s predictability under unexpected scenarios is engineered. A good harness here means a sponsor can guess what the worker will do before something unusual happens.

Cognition answers how does it learn, remember, and consult? Memory, in the task-agent harness, is a store. In the wider harness, cognition is a structure: a hierarchy of sources of truth, an explicit conflict-resolution order, a learning loop closed against outcomes the worker can observe over time. A worker that recommends investment decisions needs to know its scoring model is calibrated against passes it has made. A worker that runs capacity allocation needs to remember that last Monday’s divergence between its proposal and the human’s decision is data for next Monday’s tuning. None of that is the model. All of it is the harness.

Governance answers how is it managed and held to account? This is the layer the task-agent literature has thought about least, and the layer that distinguishes a digital worker in production from a demo that survives a quarter. A worker without a named owner, a measurable KPI, and a contingency works — until the day it doesn’t. The harness here includes a service account dedicated per worker, audit trails that distinguish worker actions from human actions, a documented retirement path, and a pause mechanism the sponsor can pull without engineering on call.

Six layers, six questions, one page. The vocabulary is stable because the questions are old. What is new is treating them as engineering surface.

Field notes

The discipline transfers under load.

In one implementation, a single digital worker owns the capacity-management function of a several-thousand-person services firm. Five capabilities, one human sponsor, three phases of autonomy progressed by metric — not calendar — over twelve weeks. The worker proposes, the human approves in Phase 0, the human approves by exception in Phase 1, the worker executes within a defined envelope in Phase 2. The 6D canvas is the artifact that gates production: a single page, six rows, no gaps.

In another, five digital workers operate the functions of a venture capital firm — investment pipeline, portfolio stewardship, capital raising, reporting, decision intelligence. Each worker owns an entire function, with its own sponsor, its own KPIs, its own progression criteria. The waves of implementation are sequenced so the first capability of each worker reaches Phase 0 before the next worker enters the build queue. The same canvas, filled five times.

Different functions, different firms, different scales. What stays constant is the harness shape.

Closing

Prompt engineering became harness engineering when we stopped pretending the model was the product. Harness engineering becomes something larger when we stop pretending the agent is the product.

The product is the function — the recurring outcome a human used to own and now a digital worker owns alongside a human. Engineering the wider harness is the work that makes that possible.

In some teams, this is already the daily job. The vocabulary is the part still catching up.

Daniel Braz is a CTO and practitioner-researcher focused on agentic software engineering, spec-driven development, and AI-assisted workflows.

The wider harness was originally published in Level Up Coding on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: The missing step in Agentic Software Engineering: why your Agents need an exploration phase before…
items.description:
The missing step in Agentic Software Engineering: why your Agents need an exploration phase before writing specs

In spec-driven development with AI agents, the quality of the output is bounded by the quality of the specification. But where does specification quality come from? Not from the prompt — from the codebase itself.

The spec is only as good as what you know

If you’ve been working with coding agents — Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Roo Code, or any of the growing roster — you’ve likely encountered a frustrating pattern. You write a clear, detailed specification. The agent executes it competently. The code compiles. The tests pass. And yet, something is wrong.

Maybe a legacy validation function still runs on every request, now redundant after your refactor. Maybe a feature flag check was duplicated rather than consolidated. Maybe an event handler that should have been updated was quietly left behind, still emitting telemetry for a flow that no longer exists.

These are not hallucination problems. They are not context window problems. They are discovery problems. The agent faithfully implemented what you specified — but the specification didn’t capture the full surface area of the change.

After months of practicing Specification-Driven Development (SDD) across real product teams, I’ve come to a clear conclusion: between the human’s intent and the written specification, there is a missing step. I call it the Exploration Phase — and skipping it is the single most common source of subtle, hard-to-catch defects in agentic workflows.

Two failure modes that specs alone can’t prevent

When an agent works from an incomplete specification against a complex codebase, the failures cluster into two predictable categories.

Failure mode 1: incomplete implementation

This is the more obvious one. The spec says “update the payment processing flow to support the new tax calculation,” and the agent does exactly that — in the main PaymentService. But the codebase also has a LegacyPaymentAdapter that wraps the old flow for backward compatibility, a PaymentValidator that asserts on the old tax field structure, and a batch reconciliation job that processes payments offline using its own copy of the calculation logic.

The agent didn’t ignore these. It never knew they existed. And why would it? The spec didn’t mention them, and the agent’s initial file discovery — typically a recursive grep or a quick codebase scan — found the primary service and stopped there.

Failure mode 2: residual artifacts

This one is subtler and often more dangerous. When you modify or replace existing functionality, the old implementation doesn’t just need updating — parts of it need to be removed. Dead code, orphaned imports, unused configuration entries, obsolete test fixtures, deprecated API routes, stale environment variables.

Agents are remarkably good at adding code. They are significantly worse at recognizing what should be deleted. This isn’t a capability limitation — it’s an information asymmetry problem. To know what to remove, you need to understand what existed before, why it existed, and whether its reason for being has been invalidated by the change. That understanding requires deliberate exploration, not just implementation.

The result is a codebase that slowly accumulates sediment — layers of dead code that increase cognitive load, confuse future agents (and humans), and occasionally cause actual bugs when dormant code paths are accidentally triggered.

What the exploration phase actually is

The Exploration Phase is a structured, agent-assisted investigation of the codebase that happens before you write the specification. Its purpose is to build a change impact map — a comprehensive understanding of every file, function, configuration entry, and test that will be affected by the intended change.

Think of it as the agentic equivalent of what a senior developer does instinctively when they get a new task on a codebase they know well. They don’t start coding. They mentally trace the impact. “If I change this, what else touches it? What depends on this interface? Where is this consumed? Are there cron jobs, background workers, or admin panels that use this?”

The problem is that agentic workflows skip this step. The human writes a spec based on their understanding (which may be incomplete), and the agent goes straight to implementation. The Exploration Phase formalizes that missing investigation.

Here is how it works in practice.

Implementing the exploration phase

Step 1: frame the change as a question, not an instruction

The first interaction with the agent should not be “implement X.” It should be “help me understand everything in this codebase that will be affected if I change X.”

Here is a real prompt template I use:

I'm planning a change to [brief description of the change].
Before writing the implementation spec, I need you to perform
an exploration of the codebase to map the full impact surface.

Specifically:
1. Identify every file that directly implements or references
   the current behavior I'm changing
2. Identify every file that *depends on* the current behavior
   (consumers, validators, serializers, tests, configuration)
3. Identify any code that may become *obsolete* after this
   change — dead code paths, unused imports, orphaned config,
   deprecated helpers
4. Flag any implicit coupling — shared state, event buses,
   database triggers, cron jobs, or background workers that
   interact with this functionality

Output format: structured list grouped by category, with file
paths and a one-line description of why each is relevant.

The key insight here is that you’re asking the agent to investigate, not implement. This changes its behavior fundamentally. Instead of searching for the minimum set of files needed to make a change, it searches for the maximum set of files that could be affected.

Step 2: validate and expand the map

The agent’s first pass will be good but rarely complete. This is where the human-in-the-loop adds irreplaceable value. Review the impact map and ask targeted follow-up questions:

Good. Now check specifically:

- Are there any API routes that expose the old behavior
  to external consumers?
- Are there feature flags or environment variables that
  control this behavior?
- Are there any database migrations or seed data that
  reference the old structure?
- Are there integration tests or E2E tests that assert
  on the current behavior?
- Is this functionality documented anywhere (README,
  API docs, Swagger/OpenAPI specs)?

This second pass typically uncovers 20–40% more affected surface area. These are precisely the files that would have been silently missed during implementation.

Step 3: classify the impact

Once the map is complete, ask the agent to classify each item:

For each item in the impact map, classify it as:

- MODIFY: needs to be updated to reflect the new behavior
- DELETE: will become obsolete and should be removed
- VERIFY: may or may not need changes — needs human review
- ADJACENT: not directly affected but should be regression-tested

This classification will form the basis of the implementation spec.

This classification step is what transforms raw exploration into actionable specification input. It is particularly critical for the DELETE category — without explicit classification, the agent will default to leaving everything in place.

Step 4: write the spec from the impact map

Now — and only now — do you write the specification. The difference is transformative. Instead of a spec that describes what to build, you have a spec that describes what to build, what to modify, what to remove, and what to verify. The spec becomes a complete change manifest rather than a forward-looking instruction set.

A spec section derived from exploration looks qualitatively different:

## Files to Modify
- `src/services/PaymentService.ts` — replace tax calculation logic
- `src/adapters/LegacyPaymentAdapter.ts` — update wrapper to use new interface
- `src/validators/PaymentValidator.ts` — update assertion to new field structure
- `src/jobs/ReconciliationJob.ts` — update offline calculation to match 

## Files to Delete
- `src/utils/oldTaxHelpers.ts` — all functions now replaced by new module
- `src/config/tax-tables-v1.json` — superseded by v2 config
- `test/fixtures/legacy-tax-scenarios.json` — test data for removed code path 

## Files to Verify (Human Review)
- `src/middleware/auditLog.ts` — may log old field names
- `docs/api/payment-endpoints.md` — may reference old behavior 

## Regression Test Surface
- `test/e2e/payment-flow.spec.ts`
- `test/integration/reconciliation.spec.ts`

Compare this to a typical pre-exploration spec: “Update the payment flow to use the new tax calculation module. Ensure backward compatibility. Update tests.” The information density difference is stark — and that difference directly determines the quality of the agent’s output.

Why agents don’t do this naturally

A reasonable question is: why can’t the agent just do this exploration on its own as part of implementation? The answer is nuanced.

First, agents optimize for task completion. When given an implementation instruction, they search for the minimum viable set of changes to satisfy it. Exploration is divergent — it intentionally expands the search space rather than converging on a solution. These are fundamentally different cognitive modes, and mixing them in a single prompt leads to shallow exploration.

Second, context window economics work against thorough exploration during implementation. Every file the agent reads consumes tokens that could be used for reasoning about the actual code changes. In practice, agents facing complex implementations will prune their file reading to stay within context limits — and the pruned files are precisely the peripheral ones that exploration would have surfaced.

Third, the DELETE classification requires negative reasoning — understanding that something’s absence is significant. This is inherently harder than positive reasoning (“this file needs to change”) and requires a dedicated analytical frame that implementation mode doesn’t provide.

The exploration phase in the SDD workflow

For those familiar with my earlier work on Specification-Driven Development, the Exploration Phase slots in as Phase 0 — the step that precedes spec authoring:

Intent → Exploration → Specification → Implementation → Validation
         (Phase 0)     (Phase 1)       (Phase 2)        (Phase 3)

It does not replace any existing phase. It enriches the specification phase by ensuring the spec is written from a position of comprehensive knowledge rather than partial assumption. The time investment is modest — typically 10 to 20 minutes for a well-scoped change — and the reduction in rework, missed deletions, and post-merge defects more than justifies it.

In teams I work with, we’ve seen the Exploration Phase reduce “spec-to-merge revision cycles” — the number of times a spec or implementation needs correction after the agent’s first pass — by roughly 50%. More importantly, it almost eliminates the class of bugs caused by orphaned code and incomplete refactors, which are among the hardest to catch in code review because they involve noticing the absence of a change.

The human-in-the-loop is not optional

It would be tempting to fully automate the Exploration Phase — to build a pipeline where the agent explores, classifies, generates the spec, and implements, all without human intervention. I would caution strongly against this.

The Exploration Phase is where human judgment adds the most leverage in the entire SDD pipeline. The agent is excellent at mechanical discovery — tracing imports, finding references, mapping dependencies. But the human brings institutional knowledge, architectural intent, and the ability to recognize when something should exist but doesn’t, or when a coupling is intentional versus accidental.

This is the step where a senior engineer’s intuition — “wait, doesn’t the billing system also touch this?” — gets formalized into the specification. Without the human validation pass, you’re simply automating incomplete discovery at a larger scale.

Practical takeaways

If you’re using coding agents for anything beyond greenfield feature development, here are the concrete steps to adopt:

Start every modification task with exploration, not implementation. Change your first prompt from “implement this” to “help me understand everything this change will affect.”

Make DELETE explicit in every spec. If your specification only lists what to create and modify, it is incomplete by definition. Every modification to existing functionality should have an explicit “files to remove” section.

Budget time for the second pass. The agent’s first exploration sweep is a starting point. The targeted follow-up questions are where the high-value discoveries happen.

Treat the impact map as a deliverable. It’s not a throwaway artifact — it’s the foundation of your spec and a valuable record for code review. When reviewers can see the exploration map alongside the implementation, review quality improves dramatically.

The Exploration Phase is not a radical idea. It’s what experienced engineers have always done, internalized so deeply that it became invisible. The advent of agentic workflows has made it visible again — because the agents don’t have that internalized knowledge, and they need us to provide it explicitly.

The question is not whether your agents need this information. They do. The question is whether you provide it before the spec or discover its absence after the implementation. One of those paths is significantly cheaper than the other.

Daniel Braz is CTO at BRQ Product & Experience Studios, where he leads digital product engineering and applied AI research. He writes about specification-driven development, agentic workflows, and the evolving role of human judgment in AI-assisted software engineering.

The missing step in Agentic Software Engineering: why your Agents need an exploration phase before… was originally published in Level Up Coding on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: The compiler was never the right analogy
items.description:
A claim has been making the rounds: reading the code an LLM produces is no different from reading the assembly a compiler produces. The argument usually arrives with a citation — some paper from the late seventies or early eighties, back when serious people still debated whether compilers could be trusted, and whether the responsible thing was to inspect the output of every translation pass before shipping it. The implication is that we’re living through the same transition, one abstraction layer up. We learned to stop reading assembly. We will learn to stop reading the code our agents write.

It’s a tidy story, and the way it’s wrong is more instructive than the way it’s right.

The compiler debate of the seventies was about trust, but the trust in question had a specific shape. By the time FORTRAN had been in production for fifteen years and PL/I was shipping, nobody seriously doubted that compilers could produce correct output in principle — that had been settled by a combination of formal arguments, exhaustive testing, and the sheer fact that working programs kept being delivered. The open question was whether the optimizations the compiler was now performing — instruction scheduling, register allocation, dead-code elimination — preserved the semantics of the source program in every case. People published worked examples of compilers miscompiling correct code. The conservative answer, for a while, was: read the assembly, especially in the hot path.

We eventually stopped doing that, and we stopped for a specific reason. Not because compilers became magically reliable, but because the relationship between C and assembly is, in principle, auditable. The C standard defines a semantics. Assembly has a semantics. The compiler’s job is to preserve the first inside the second. When something goes wrong, you can — at least in the limit case — point to where it went wrong. Xavier Leroy’s CompCert, which actually proves this preservation for a meaningful subset of C, is the formal completion of an argument we already half-trusted in practice.

The compiler is a function. It has a domain and a codomain, both of them formal. You can argue about whether the function is correctly implemented; you cannot argue about what it would mean for it to be correct.

This is what breaks the analogy with LLMs.

When I prompt an agent with “build a checkout button that handles partial refunds,” I am not handing it a specification in any technical sense. I am handing it a request expressed in a natural language that has no formal semantics, and the agent is producing an artifact in a language that does. The mapping between the two is interpretive. There is no equivalence theorem available, even in principle, because there is nothing on the input side to be equivalent to. Two runs of the same prompt will produce different code, and both can be reasonable, because “reasonable” is not a formal property of natural-language requests.

This isn’t peripheral — it’s where the problem lives.

The compiler-trust debate was about confirming that a known transformation between two formal artifacts had been performed correctly. The LLM-trust question is about confirming that an interpretation of an informal request resulted in code that does what the interpreter thought you meant. These are different categories of problem, and the techniques that resolved the first — formal semantics, equivalence proofs, exhaustive test suites, decades of empirical hardening — do not apply to the second in the form we’d need.

There’s a temptation, when this gets pointed out, to push back by saying: well, then we just need to make the input formal. Write better specs. Be more precise. This is the move I find most revealing, because it’s the same move that gives spec-driven development its appeal, and it carries the same mistake. Intent is not a deficient version of specification. It’s a different kind of object, one that resists formalization not by accident but because the things people actually want from software — including the parts they’ll only discover during the build — are constitutively informal. You cannot specify your way out of this. The compiler analogy treats the prompt as if it were already a program in a language we just haven’t formalized yet.

The honest version of the comparison is narrower. There is a layer at which we will stop reading LLM output the way we stopped reading assembly, and that layer is the boilerplate one — where the mapping from request to code is constrained enough that we can treat the agent as a deterministic-enough function for practical purposes. CRUD endpoints. Standard refactors. Migrations between idioms. For these, the compiler analogy approximately holds, because we’ve narrowed the input until it behaves like a formal language.

For everything else — the work that actually matters, the part where the thinking happens — we are not in the compiler era. We are somewhere older and harder, in the part of programming that was never about translation in the first place.

Daniel Braz is a CTO and practitioner-researcher focused on agentic software engineering, spec-driven development, and AI-assisted workflows. He writes about the engineering realities behind the AI hype.

The compiler was never the right analogy was originally published in Artificial Intelligence in Plain English on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: The engineer’s new flow: specifying and coding in parallel with AI Agents
items.description:
Every senior developer I talk to has the same story. They started using AI coding agents — Cursor, Copilot, Claude Code — and the first week felt like a superpower. Code appeared faster than they could think. The second month, a pattern emerged: the code came fast, but so did the rework. Features that looked done kept coming back. Edge cases slipped through. The architecture drifted in ways no one intended.

The problem isn’t the agent. The problem is what you’re feeding it.

The Missing Artifact

Most developers interact with AI agents through prompts — natural language descriptions of what they want. Sometimes it’s a Jira ticket, sometimes a quick sentence in the chat window. The agent takes that input, generates code, and the developer reviews, tweaks, ships. This works beautifully for small, self-contained tasks. It falls apart at the first sign of real complexity.

Here’s why. Every time you give an agent a prompt, you’re asking it to reconstruct the full context of your problem from a few sentences. The business rules, the architectural constraints, the deployment requirements, the edge cases — all of that needs to exist somewhere. If it only exists in your head, the agent will guess. And it will guess confidently.

What’s missing is a specification. Not a waterfall-era requirements document that takes weeks to write and is outdated before the first sprint. A living, co-created spec that captures decisions as you make them — and gives the agent something solid to anchor to.

The Spec Is Not Written Before the Code. It Moves With It.

This is where the argument diverges from the old waterfall playbook. I’m not suggesting you spend two weeks writing a spec before touching code. I’m also not suggesting you write the full spec and then hand it to a coding agent. Both of those models put the engineer in the wrong posture — passive, separated from the work.

What I’m describing is a pipeline. You specify a small block — a single function’s behavior, one API contract, a set of business rules for a specific flow. That block is sharp enough to hand to the coding agent. While the agent generates code for that block, you’re already working on the next one. You’re specifying block two while block one is being built. When block one comes back, you validate it — does it match the spec? — and move on. Meanwhile, block three is taking shape in your conversation with the agent.

This is what flow feels like in spec-driven development. You’re not watching. You’re not waiting. You are continuously making decisions and feeding them forward, always one or two blocks ahead of the code. The spec and the code advance together, interleaved, with the engineer driving the pace.

The critical distinction: in vibe coding, the conversation produces code directly. In spec-driven development, the conversation produces decisions first — but those decisions don’t pile up in a document you’ll use later. They flow immediately into implementation, block by block. The spec is never “finished.” It’s always just ahead of the code, like headlights on a road.

Three Lenses, One Engineer

Not every dimension of a problem needs the same kind of thinking. A business rule is a different animal from a deployment constraint, and both are different from an architectural trade-off. Trying to think about all three simultaneously leads to shallow coverage of each.

This is where specialized agents earn their place. Instead of one general-purpose coding agent, the engineer works with three distinct lenses:

The PM/PD agent thinks in terms of users, journeys, and business logic. When you engage it, you’re asking: what problem are we solving? What are the edge cases from the user’s perspective? What happens when things go wrong — what does the user see? What business rules govern this behavior, and are there exceptions? This lens produces the functional layer of the spec — acceptance criteria, user-facing states, business rule definitions.

The Architect agent thinks in terms of trade-offs, patterns, and contracts between components. It asks: how does this fit into the existing system? What are the interface boundaries? Where does complexity live, and is that the right place for it? What are we trading off — consistency for availability, simplicity for flexibility? This lens produces the structural layer — component boundaries, data flows, API contracts, technical constraints.

The DevOps agent thinks in terms of production reality. It asks: how does this get deployed without breaking what’s running? What needs to be observable? What happens at 10x traffic? What’s the rollback path? This lens produces the operational layer — deployment strategy, monitoring requirements, scaling considerations, failure modes.

The engineer doesn’t execute these in a rigid sequence. You start wherever your intuition pulls you — whichever dimension feels most uncertain, most risky, or most foundational for this particular problem. Sometimes the business logic is fuzzy and everything else depends on clarifying it first. Sometimes you already know exactly what but not how — so you start with architecture. Sometimes it’s a hotfix and the first question is “how does this reach production safely in the next hour.”

Once the first lens begins to sharpen, the others don’t wait politely in line. They start reacting. Architectural decisions reveal business edge cases you hadn’t considered. Deployment constraints eliminate architectural options you were exploring. The PM/PD lens revisits scope once the operational cost of a feature becomes concrete. The lenses run in parallel, slightly offset — one always a little ahead, pulling the others forward, each adjusting as new information surfaces.

Small Steps Compound. Big Steps Collapse.

There’s an operational principle underneath all of this: smaller blocks of work produce better results than larger ones. This isn’t just good engineering practice — it’s a direct consequence of how AI agents handle context.

An agent that receives “implement the entire payment module” will start strong and degrade as it works. Context drifts. Early decisions get contradicted by later ones. The output looks complete but is internally inconsistent. An agent that receives “implement the payment validation function with these five acceptance criteria” stays anchored. The scope is small enough that the context doesn’t degrade before the task is done.

This is why the pipeline works. Each spec block is small by design — a micro-contract with its own scope, criteria, and constraints. The coding agent handles one at a time. The engineer validates each output before the next block lands. Every handoff is a checkpoint where the human re-anchors the agent to the original intent.

Ten small, validated steps produce far more reliable software than one large, impressive-looking generation. It feels slower per step, but it’s faster to production — because you’re not spending three days debugging the output of a single ambitious prompt.

The Engineer as Orchestrator

In this model, the engineer’s job changes — but it doesn’t shrink. If anything, it demands a broader skill set. You need enough product sense to challenge the PM/PD agent when it hallucinates a business rule that sounds plausible but doesn’t exist. You need enough architectural judgment to recognize when the Architect agent is over-engineering a solution. You need enough operational awareness to know when the DevOps agent is being too conservative or not conservative enough.

The agent proposes. The engineer decides. That’s the dynamic. The agents bring speed and breadth — they can explore a solution space faster than you can alone. But they can’t resolve the tensions between lenses. When the PM/PD agent wants a flexible user experience that creates architectural complexity, and the DevOps agent flags that complexity as an operational risk — that’s a judgment call. That’s yours.

And because spec and code are interleaved, you’re making these calls continuously — not in a planning session days before implementation, but in the middle of the work, with real code taking shape around your decisions. You see the consequences of a spec choice within minutes, not weeks. That feedback loop is what keeps the spec honest and the code aligned.

A Walkthrough: Building a Conditional Checkout Flow

Let’s make this concrete. You’re tasked with adding a conditional discount system to an e-commerce checkout. Discounts depend on cart value, user tier, and active promotions — and they can stack, but with caps.

You look at the problem and your gut says: architecture first. You already know the checkout has a pricing engine, and wherever this discount logic lands will shape everything else. So you start with the Architect agent. You describe the existing system, the pricing engine’s interfaces, and the new requirement. The agent proposes two options: extend the pricing engine or build a standalone discount service. You push back — a standalone service adds a network hop and a failure mode for something that should be a core pricing concern. The agent adjusts, proposes an extension pattern with a DiscountResolver interface plugged into the existing engine. That feels right. You now have a structural decision: the discount logic lives inside the pricing engine, behind a clear interface.

That first block of spec — the component boundary and the interface contract — is sharp enough. You hand it to the coding agent: scaffold the DiscountResolver interface and integrate it into the pricing engine's calculation pipeline. While the agent builds that, you shift to the dimension that just became urgent.

Because now you need to know: what are the actual rules this interface needs to implement? You open a session with the PM/PD agent. You describe the feature: user tiers, cart-value thresholds, stackable promotions with caps. The agent starts probing — what tiers exist? Can a user hold multiple promotions simultaneously? What does the user see when a discount is partially applied versus fully applied versus capped? You work through three rounds and produce a decision table for discount stacking, a set of business rules for tier resolution, and acceptance criteria for each user-visible state. Some of these rules you already knew. Others — like the edge case where a promotion expires between cart load and checkout confirmation — the agent surfaced by asking the right question.

Midway through that PM/PD conversation, the coding agent finishes the scaffold. You pause, validate it — the interface is clean, the integration point is correct. Good. You take two of the business rules you’ve already nailed down with the PM/PD agent and write the next spec block: implement tier-based discount calculation with these acceptance criteria. The coding agent starts working. You go back to finish the PM/PD session.

With business rules crystallized, a thought hits you: the discount calculation depends on the promotion service. What if it’s down during a flash sale? You bring this to the DevOps agent, sharing the architectural decision (discount logic lives in the pricing engine) and the business rules (promotions are fetched in real-time). The agent flags the dependency immediately: no cache, no fallback, single point of failure during peak load. It proposes a caching layer with a degradation path — expired cache defaults to no-promotion pricing rather than blocking checkout. You adjust the TTL based on your knowledge of how often promotions actually change. The DevOps agent also defines monitoring: alert on promotion service latency, track discount application rates for anomaly detection.

But this operational decision feeds back. If there’s a cache, the pricing engine needs to know whether it’s working with live or cached data — that changes the interface you already specced. You go back to the Architect agent: the DiscountResolver needs a data freshness indicator so downstream logic can flag "discount may not reflect latest promotions" in the UI. That architectural change creates a new PM/PD question — what does the user see when discount data is stale? You open that thread, nail down the copy and the UX behavior, and produce another spec block.

This is the rhythm. Spec a block, hand it off, spec the next. Lenses sharpen each other — architecture exposes operational risks, operational constraints reshape architecture, both surface business questions nobody had asked. The engineer moves between them based on what needs attention now, not based on a predetermined sequence.

By the end, you haven’t written a spec document. You’ve made a series of decisions — about business logic, about component structure, about operational resilience — and each one flowed into code within minutes of being made. The spec exists as a trail of micro-contracts, each validated against working code. The feature is built, and it’s built on decisions you understand because you made every one of them.

Total time: maybe half a day. Features built this way tend to stay built.

Why This Matters Now

The tooling for AI-assisted development is improving at an extraordinary pace. Agents are getting better at generating code, understanding context, and working with larger codebases. This creates a tempting vision: fully autonomous agents that pick up tickets from a backlog, generate code, open pull requests, and only need a human to click “approve.”

That vision has a structural flaw. It assumes that a ticket carries enough context for an agent to make good decisions — about business logic, about architecture, about operational trade-offs. In practice, tickets capture intent at best, and even that intent is often incomplete. The more autonomous the agent, the more it needs a rich, structured spec to compensate for the absence of a human in the loop. Without it, you get code that compiles, passes superficial review, and fails in ways that only surface in production.

But here’s the deeper issue: even if you could write a perfect spec upfront and hand it to an autonomous agent, you’d lose the feedback loop that makes the spec right. In the model I’ve described, the engineer discovers problems in the spec because code is being generated alongside it. The scaffold reveals an interface gap. The implementation surfaces an edge case. The deployment constraint reshapes the architecture. That interplay between specification and implementation — that continuous correction — is what produces software that holds up. Remove the human from the loop, and you don’t just lose oversight. You lose the mechanism that makes the decisions good in the first place.

The question isn’t whether agents will become more capable — they will. The question is where the human adds irreplaceable value. The answer, I believe, is in the specification layer: the structured decisions that tell an agent not just what to build, but why this approach, why these constraints, why this trade-off and not that one. The engineer who masters spec-driven development doesn’t compete with the agent for speed. They operate at a different altitude — one where judgment, context, and cross-domain thinking compound into software that actually works.

The investment hasn’t disappeared. It’s shifted from writing code to making decisions. And that, for a senior engineer, should feel less like a loss and more like coming home.

Daniel Braz is CTO at BRQ Product & Experience Studios, where he leads a digital products studio focused on AI-augmented software engineering. He is currently researching context degradation and specification-driven development in agentic workflows.

The engineer’s new flow: specifying and coding in parallel with AI Agents was originally published in Level Up Coding on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.
